metadata.title: Blockchain Fundamentals: Internet Computing and Decentralization Knowledge Graph
metadata.description: Concept dependency graph for Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture 1 — 11 nodes, 14 edges
metadata.source: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture / Chris Dixon 'Why Decentralization Matters'

nodes.name: Evolution of the Web: 1.0 → 2.0 → 3.0
nodes.description: Understand the 3-stage evolution of the Web, the transition from Stateless to Stateful, and the ownership revolution.
key_ideas: Web 1.0 (read) → Web 2.0 (read+write, Stateless) → Web 3.0 (read+write+own, Stateful)
Limitations of Stateless Protocol and the source of platform power
Open Source → Open Resource transition
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01

nodes.name: Blockchain Fundamentals
nodes.description: Learn the basic structure of blockchain, asymmetric cryptography, hash functions, and State Machine concepts.
key_ideas: Blockchain = A tamper-proof ledger shared globally
Chain formation through asymmetric cryptography and hash functions
EOA and private key signing principles
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01

nodes.name: Consensus Mechanisms: PoW vs PoS
nodes.description: Learn the principles of PoW and PoS, The Merge, Nothing at Stake problem, and Slashing.
key_ideas: PoW: Earn block creation rights through proof of work (computation)
PoS: Earn block creation rights through staking coins
The Merge (2022): 99.95% energy reduction
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01

nodes.name: Double Spending Problem and Necessity of Consensus
nodes.description: Learn the essence of double spending, why time-based ordering doesn't work, and how PoW/PoS solve it.
key_ideas: Double spending: Attempting to pay twice with the same money
The problem of determining order without a central clock
Solution by qualification: Having only one person record
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture

nodes.name: Lifecycle of Centralized Platforms
nodes.description: Learn Chris Dixon's 3-stage lifecycle of centralized platforms and the Google case study.
key_ideas: Three stages: Attract → Grow → Extract
Positive-sum → Zero-sum transition
Google: 'Don't be evil' → Antitrust ruling

nodes.name: Bait-and-Switch: Platform Betrayal
nodes.description: Learn the concept of bait-and-switch, historical examples, and its consequences.
key_ideas: Bait: Attract with free usage
Switch: Change rules, impose fees
Result: Developers avoid centralized platforms

nodes.name: Voice and Exit: Solutions Through Decentralization
nodes.description: Learn Voice (voting rights) and Exit (right to leave), the concept of Fork, and how decentralization prevents bait-and-switch.
key_ideas: Voice: Ability to change rules through governance voting
Exit: Ability to leave without cost, Fork is possible
Code is law, open source, fork threat, token incentives

nodes.name: Wikipedia vs Encarta: Triumph of Decentralization
nodes.description: Learn the Wikipedia and Encarta case study, dynamic process comparison, and the lesson of late blooming.
key_ideas: Centralized: High initial quality → improves only at employee speed
Decentralized: Initially incomplete → explosive community growth
Late blooming (大器晚成): Great vessels are completed slowly
nodes.paper_ref: Chris Dixon, 'Why Decentralization Matters' (2018) / 道德經 Chapter 41

nodes.name: Layer 2: Solving Scalability
nodes.description: Learn the blockchain trilemma, Layer 2 principles, and Optimistic/ZK Rollups.
key_ideas: Blockchain trilemma: Decentralization, Security, Scalability
L2: Batch transactions, record only results on L1
Optimistic Rollups vs ZK-Rollups
nodes.paper_ref: Chris Dixon, 'Why Decentralization Matters' (2018) / Ethereum L2 Docs

nodes.name: DeFi and NFTs: The Blockchain Ecosystem
nodes.description: Learn DeFi concepts and history, NFTs, CBDC, and lessons from the FTX collapse.
key_ideas: DeFi: Financial services without central institutions
NFT: Proof of digital ownership
FTX collapse: Proof of why decentralization matters
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 02

nodes.name: Ethereum and Smart Contracts
nodes.description: Learn Ethereum's innovation, EVM, Gas, and smart contract principles.
key_ideas: Ethereum: Turing-complete smart contracts
EVM: A virtual machine that runs identically worldwide
Gas: Computational cost, defense against malicious code

edges.description: Web3 Stateful concept is the foundation for understanding blockchain
Blockchain basics are prerequisite for understanding consensus mechanisms
Consensus mechanisms solve the double spending problem
Blockchain basics are prerequisite for understanding Ethereum
Web 2.0 platform limitations are the background for lifecycle theory
Bait-and-switch problem raises the need for Voice/Exit solutions
Voice/Exit is the mechanism for decentralization's victory
Understanding consensus mechanisms is prerequisite for L2
L2 enables expansion of DeFi/NFT ecosystem
Smart contracts are the technical foundation of DeFi/NFT
Solving double spending contributes to understanding trust structures
Decentralization victory principles are the basis for DeFi/NFT vision
Platform problem awareness explains the need for DeFi/NFT
